name: Gefion
description: Gefion is a goddess in Norse mythology associated with virginity, plowing, agriculture, and prosperity. She is primarily known for her clever trickery in acquiring land from the Swedish king Gylfi. According to the Prose Edda, Gefion visited Gylfi disguised as a vagrant woman and entertained him with stories. As a reward, Gylfi offered her as much land as she could plow in a single day and night. Gefion then revealed her divine nature and transformed her four sons (born from a giant) into oxen, who plowed a massive chunk of land out of Sweden, creating the island of Zealand in Denmark. The hole left behind became Lake Mälaren in Sweden. She is also considered a protector of virgins, and some sources link her to fertility and abundance. Gefion's association with agriculture connects her to the earth's bounty and the cycle of growth. She is often invoked for good fortune and successful harvests. Though not one of the most prominent deities in the Norse pantheon, her story highlights the power of cunning and divine intervention in shaping the landscape and ensuring prosperity. She embodies both feminine strength and resourcefulness, demonstrating the ability to acquire and cultivate resources for the benefit of her people. Her origins are somewhat ambiguous, with conflicting accounts regarding her parentage, but her role as a powerful goddess of the earth and its riches remains consistent.
subtitle: Goddess of Virginity, Plowing, and Prosperity

keyMyths.title: Gefion and King Gylfi
keyMyths.description: Gefion tricks King Gylfi of Sweden into giving her as much land as she can plow in a day and night. She transforms her sons into oxen and creates Zealand.

keyMyths.title: Gefion's Virginity
keyMyths.description: Gefion is associated with virginity, and some stories suggest she remains a virgin goddess. This aspect links her to purity and untouched land.

keyMyths.title: Gefion and Agriculture
keyMyths.description: Gefion's connection to plowing and agriculture makes her a goddess of fertility and abundance. She is invoked for successful harvests.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: Primary source for Gefion's myth, particularly the story of Zealand's creation.
